signal soup

We introduce Signal Soup, a simple model emphasizing "loose" code alignment —
a Gedankenexperiment formalized for in silico simulation.

Lexical coordination models assume…

  • Arbitrariness — no motivated link between the two planes (Saussure 1916)
  • Direct feedback — success is evaluated through immediate sender/receiver exchange
  • Dyadic relationship — communication modelled as pairs, even in larger groups
  • Optimal alignment — ideal communication = complete code sharing among all participants
01

Signal Soup —
the model

CEnv + CAgs

Communicative environment

  • Communication = interaction among CAgs mediated by a shared communicative environment (CEnv)
  • Signal — 3-letter word, lowercase alphabet — 26³ = 17,576 possible signals
  • Agents process and replace signals
  • No direct agent-to-agent exchange
CAg structure

Communicative agent

  • Detector → Effector — 3-letter signal types (Holland 1995)
  • Sign function sf — mapping d → e
  • Relevance r — score per sf, capped at rmax = 10
  • Memory — sfmax = 10 sign functions per agent
The algorithm
02

The Band

The Band

  • Band — n CAgs sharing a single CEnv  |  CEnv size = n
  • Initialisation — each CAg gets 1 sf (random d → e), with r = 0 (easily replaced)
  • CEnv seed — one effector signal per agent; agents start by emitting their only output
  • Asynchronous — at each turn, one randomly selected CAg inspects CEnv and acts
03

What emerges

fig. 2 CAg topology after 2,000 turns — loops, chains, synonymy emerge spontaneously  ·  white = detectors · black = effectors · red = both
fig. 3 Band–CEnv matching relations — 20 agents, 2,000 turns
definition

Type-token ratio  =  distinct signal types  /  total signal occurrences in CEnv

ratio = 1  →  all signals distinct  ·  ratio → 0  →  high redundancy

fig. 4 Type-token ratio → 1/3  ·  avg sf/agent → ~5.6  ·  10 band sizes, 100 runs each, 10,000 turns
fig. 5 Band topology after 2,000 turns — red edges: shared sf (label = agent count)

Partial alignment

  • After 2,000 turns: ~26% of sign functions shared by 2+ agents
  • Some shared by up to 7 agents — without any centralised coordination
  • Majority of sf remain idiosyncratic — each agent keeps its own repertoire
  • Eco: not a dictionary (shared code) but an encyclopedia (distributed competence)
04

Discussion

Three assumptions — revisited

  • Direct feedback  →  none — agents act on CEnv, never receive success/failure signals
  • Dyadic  →  pairs, dialogical  vs  n agents, polylogical
  • Optimal alignment  →  complete code sharing  vs  sharedness — partial overlap

Stigmergy

  • Agents coordinate indirectly — by modifying and responding to CEnv
  • No direct agent-to-agent exchange — the environment is the medium and the message
  • Priming through repeated exposure: signals that appear often get reinforced (Pickering & Garrod 2004)
  • Local interactions → global patterns  (Theraulaz & Bonabeau 1999)

The Band as social unit

  • Not pairs but a small, tightly integrated population — a social unit (Fitch 2010)
  • Detection and action are mandatory — every agent acts on every turn
  • This embodies Mitteilungsbedürfnis — the compulsion to communicate (Fitch 2010)
  • Akin to enforced Gricean cooperation — not chosen, but structural (Grice 1989)

Connectivity, not alignment

  • Graph connectivity + circuit formation (Steels 1997) recalls autocatalytic sets (Kauffman 2000, 2019)
  • Looping sf yield copier behaviour (Fontana 1991) — ~5.6 sf/agent is the threshold

Encyclopedia, not dictionary

  • Not a shared code but an idiosyncratic, overlapping repertoire (Eco 1986)
  • Distributed competence — sufficient for coordination, without convergence
1/3
type-token ratio
three tokens per signal type in CEnv
all band sizes · all runs
5.6
avg sign functions / agent
well below sfmax = 10
sufficient for coordination

Two emergent equilibria — why?

  • Reinforcement dynamics — frequent signals match more, get reproduced — positive feedback
  • Emergent repertoire size — ~5.6 sf is sufficient for coordination; rare signals are replaced
  • Stigmergic coordination — agent A produces xyz → raises probability agent B learns xyz
  • Result: tp:tk → 1/3  ·  sf/agent → ~5.6  ·  robust across n = 10–100
conclusions from a signal soup

Stable communication emerges without direct feedback, dyadic exchange, or complete code sharing.

Stigmergy + partial alignment = sufficient for coordination (connectivity).

Two emergent constants — tp:tk → 1/3 · sf/agent → 5.6 — mark the connectivity threshold.

Sharedness, not consensus, is the condition.
